headline: Chips Hold the Line as Selling Sweeps Most of the Market
body_html:
Tuesday's midday tape is one of clear divergence: semiconductors are the lone sector trading in the green, up roughly half a percent, while seven of eight tracked sectors are in the red heading into the afternoon. Breadth tells the fuller story — 351 names in the universe are lower against just 146 advancers, a lopsided ratio that signals broad-based pressure rather than isolated pockets of weakness. Homebuilders and industrials are bearing the heaviest damage, off more than one percent each.

The standout mover of the session is e.l.f. Beauty, surging more than eight percent to lead all gainers — a striking outlier in an otherwise bruised consumer sector. On the semiconductor side, Credo Technology and MaxLinear are each advancing more than four percent, lending support to the group's slim gains. Teradyne is also contributing, up roughly three and a half percent. The common thread: investors appear to be rotating toward chip-related connectivity and testing names as the broader tech landscape steadies.

Into the close, traders will be watching whether semiconductor strength can hold as geopolitical headlines continue to inject volatility. Energy names are sliding, with Magnolia Oil & Gas and Bloom Energy both down sharply, even as crude-related tensions escalate in the Strait of Hormuz. Any fresh headline out of the Middle East corridor could rattle sentiment before the bell.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Incident Rattles Energy Shipping Lanes
briefs.body: A tanker crew abandoned their vessel Tuesday after a reported projectile strike in the Strait of Hormuz, according to maritime-safety monitors. The incident compounds pressure on a critical global shipping corridor already strained by successive U.S. strikes on Iran. Vessel crossings through the strait have extended their recent decline in direct response to the escalating conflict.

briefs.headline: SolarEdge and Bloom Energy Lead Clean-Energy Selloff
briefs.body: SolarEdge fell more than nine percent and Bloom Energy dropped over eight percent, making them the session's hardest-hit names after semiconductors. The pair dragged the broader energy and solar complex lower amid uncertainty over global demand and geopolitical disruption to energy supply chains. No company-specific news was immediately cited for the magnitude of the moves.

briefs.headline: STAAR Surgical Posts Steepest Healthcare Decline
briefs.body: STAAR Surgical tumbled more than nine percent, the sharpest drop among all decliners tracked midday and an outlier even within a broadly weak healthcare sector. The medical-devices segment is suffering on both ends of the ledger, however — Inspire Medical Systems is simultaneously one of the session's top gainers, rising more than five percent.

briefs.headline: Chip-Stock Recovery Underpins Futures Lift at the Open
briefs.body: Wall Street futures pushed higher at the open as semiconductor shares extended a rebound that has been gathering momentum over recent sessions. Investors appeared to treat the group's resilience as a stabilizing anchor for the broader market, even as most other sectors drifted lower. Earnings season remains a parallel focus, with results continuing to shape individual stock moves.

briefs.headline: Gold Edges Higher on Faint De-escalation Hopes
briefs.body: Bullion edged up Tuesday as diplomatic overtures offered a sliver of optimism that Middle East tensions could eventually ease. The modest advance stands in contrast to equity-market jitters, underscoring gold's continued safe-haven appeal during a tenth consecutive night of U.S. military strikes against Iran. Traders are closely monitoring any shift in diplomatic signaling.

briefs.headline: Wall Street Accelerates Exit From Single-Family Rental Market
briefs.body: Institutional sellers are moving rental-home inventory at an increased pace as a legislative ban on bulk residential purchases takes effect. The shift marks a notable reversal from years of aggressive accumulation by large financial players and is being watched for potential ripple effects on local housing supply and homebuilder sentiment, a sector already down nearly two percent midday.
